narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused heavily on predictors of bar exam success drawn from past students' academic records and test scores. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 20, full-time student status, and school decile rankings from Years 1 and 3—and predicted an 84% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected; law schools use this model to prioritize applicants likely to pass the bar and succeed in practice. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking. Most influential was your Year 3 decile of 1, which is much lower than the typical student's 5.53 average—this strongly signaled to the model a dip in performance that often correlates with bar exam struggles, pushing the prediction heavily toward failure. Your LSAT score of 20, far below the dataset average of 36.70, was the second strongest factor pulling in the same direction, as lower scores like yours have historically been tied to higher failure rates. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 5—close to the average of 5.71—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing and showing early potential. Compared to typical students who pass, your later academic standing and test score were notable weak spots that outweighed your steadier start and common full-time enrollment status. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 was below the 3.21 average, it wasn't among the top drivers here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the typical range, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying once you've addressed these key areas—many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these exact improvements.
